page: Microsoft Azure M8ms is a memory-optimized virtual machine from the MS family, running on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor. It features 8 dedicated vCPUs mapped to 4 physical cores of an Intel Xeon 8280M processor operating at 2.7 GHz. The x86_64 architecture includes 32 KB L1d cache, 1.0 MB L2 cache, and 38 MB L3 cache, with support for AVX, AVX512F, and AES instructions. The server is equipped with 219.0 GB of RAM, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 27.34 GB per core, and includes 262 GB of local storage with Premium Storage capabilities.

Performance benchmarks indicate a clear tradeoff between massive memory capacity and compute speed. Single-core and multi-core CPU benchmarks, such as stress-ng and Geekbench, place the processor in the bottom 10% to 25% of tested hardware. Similarly, memory bandwidth read tests and Passmark memory scores fall into the lower 25% tier. However, the system achieves average performance in large-block memory writes at 31.88 GB/sec and Passmark extended instruction tests. Small-model LLM inference also registers average speeds, with 423.6 tokens/sec for prompt processing and 83.59 tokens/sec for text generation.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the M8ms server represents a specialized allocation of high memory density relative to its modest 8-vCPU compute footprint. It is designed for workloads requiring large in-memory datasets where raw CPU processing speed is not the primary bottleneck. This makes the instance suitable for large-scale in-memory databases, caching layers, enterprise application backends, and memory-intensive data processing tasks.
description: Microsoft Azure M8ms is a memory-optimized virtual machine configured with 8 dedicated vCPUs from an Intel Xeon 8280M processor running at 2.7 GHz, paired with 219.0 GB of RAM and 262 GB of local storage. Operating on the Microsoft Hyper-V hypervisor, this x86_64 instance provides a high memory-to-core ratio of 27.34 GB per core. Benchmark data reveals a tradeoff, with single-core and multi-core CPU performance ranking in the lower tiers, while large-block memory writes and small-model LLM inference achieve average marks. This hardware profile is qualitatively optimized for memory-dense workloads rather than raw compute speed. It is best suited for in-memory databases, caching systems, and memory-intensive enterprise applications.
